title: Business Development Manager
metaTitle: Business Development Manager Resume Examples & Tips
metaDescription: Write a winning Business Development Manager resume with proven strategies, skill keywords, and sample bullets that get interviews.
h1: How to Write a Business Development Manager Resume That Gets Interviews
intro: A strong Business Development Manager resume shows hiring managers you can identify growth opportunities, build partnerships, and close deals. We'll walk you through the exact structure, skills, and bullet points that land interviews at companies looking to scale revenue.
audience: Early-career professionals moving into BD roles, sales professionals pivoting to business development, and MBA grads targeting first BD positions.

topSkills.name: Strategic Partnership Development
topSkills.why: Hiring managers want to see you can identify and nurture high-value business relationships that drive long-term revenue.

topSkills.name: Sales Pipeline Management
topSkills.why: You need to demonstrate experience building and tracking prospects through the sales funnel using CRM tools and metrics.

topSkills.name: Market Research & Analysis
topSkills.why: BD roles require the ability to identify market trends, competitive gaps, and new customer segments to target.

topSkills.name: Revenue Growth & Forecasting
topSkills.why: Employers want proof you can predict and drive measurable revenue increases, not just activity.

topSkills.name: CRM & Sales Tools (Salesforce, HubSpot)
topSkills.why: Most BD teams use CRM software to track deals and pipelines; proficiency signals you can operate independently.

topSkills.name: Contract Negotiation
topSkills.why: BD managers close deals, so experience negotiating terms and closing contracts is a red flag for hiring managers.

topSkills.name: Cross-functional Collaboration
topSkills.why: You'll work with product, marketing, and finance teams; showing you can align and lead cross-team initiatives matters.

topSkills.name: Lead Generation & Prospecting
topSkills.why: Many BD roles start with building your own pipeline; demonstrated ability to source and qualify leads is valuable.

topSkills.name: P&L Responsibility
topSkills.why: Managing budgets and understanding profit/loss on deals or accounts shows you think like a business owner.

sampleBullets.weak: Worked with sales team to develop partnerships and increase revenue.
sampleBullets.strong: Identified and signed 12 enterprise partnership agreements with SaaS integrators, generating $2.4M in first-year revenue and establishing recurring 15% year-over-year growth.
sampleBullets.lesson: Replace vague 'partnerships' with specific deal count, revenue impact, and growth metrics that prove ROI.

sampleBullets.weak: Managed sales pipeline and tracked deals in Salesforce.
sampleBullets.strong: Owned $8M+ sales pipeline across 40+ accounts; improved deal velocity by 25% through automated pipeline reviews and weekly forecast accuracy to 92%.
sampleBullets.lesson: Show the pipeline size, action you took to improve it, and the measurable outcome (velocity, accuracy, or win rate).

sampleBullets.weak: Researched new markets and presented findings to leadership.
sampleBullets.strong: Conducted competitive analysis in 3 adjacent markets; recommended expansion into mid-market segment and led pilot that captured 8 new customers in 6 months, validating $1.2M TAM.
sampleBullets.lesson: Start with the research, but anchor it to a business decision and quantified result that followed your recommendation.

commonMistakes.fix: Replace 'attended 20 client meetings' with 'converted 6 prospects to contracts worth $450K through targeted account-based outreach.'
Every bullet should hint at dollars: quota attainment, deal size, pipeline value, or cost savings. If you didn't own revenue, reframe it as 'enabled sales team to close $3M in annual contracts.'
Mention specific industries, company types, or customer segments you've targeted (e.g., 'penetrated healthcare IT market, signed 5 hospital systems').'
Name the exact tools you've used (Salesforce, HubSpot, LinkedIn Sales Navigator, etc.) so ATS can match keywords and you signal operational readiness.
If you've sourced, negotiated, or closed partnerships, channels, or acquired accounts, lead with that—it's gold for BD hiring managers.

structureTips: Lead with a summary that highlights your biggest revenue impact and customer segment expertise (e.g., 'Business Development Manager with 4 years driving enterprise partnerships in SaaS; closed $12M+ in ACV across 25+ accounts'). Skip generic objective statements.

Put revenue and pipeline metrics above activity counts. Hiring managers scan for dollar signs first—if you grew revenue 40%, led a $5M pipeline, or acquired top 10 accounts, bury the 'number of calls made' below that.
Organize experience by outcome, not job duty. Use a structure like '[Action] → [Market/Segment] → [Result]' so each bullet tells a micro-story and stands alone.

Highlight cross-functional wins early: if you collaborated with product to launch a new offering, or worked with marketing on a go-to-market, feature that because it shows you're not just a lone wolf closer.
salarySignal: Entry-level Business Development Manager positions in the US typically range from $50K–$65K base salary plus commission; mid-level roles (3–5 years) average $70K–$90K+ base with 20–50% variable compensation, depending on industry and company size.

faq.q: Should I put sales numbers on my BD resume if I didn't hit quota?
faq.a: Yes—but reframe it as pipeline value, deals closed, or accounts acquired rather than quota %. For example, 'managed $4.2M pipeline; closed 8 deals averaging $180K ACV' is strong even if it wasn't 100% of target. Always show the context (your quota, territory, or goal) so the reader understands your contribution.

faq.q: How do I show BD impact if my previous role was mostly relationship-building with no closed deals?
faq.a: Focus on sourcing, qualification, and handoff metrics: 'sourced 30 qualified leads that generated $1.8M in closed business for sales team' or 'built network of 200+ decision-makers in target market, enabling 12 partnership conversations.' Show the downstream impact you enabled.

faq.q: What's the difference between a Business Development Manager and a Sales Manager on a resume?
faq.a: BD emphasizes strategy, partnerships, and market expansion; sales emphasizes closing and quota. On your resume, highlight new market entry, channel/partner deals, and long-term relationship building for BD roles. For sales, emphasize rep management, call quality, and individual close rate.

faq.q: Should I list CRM tools like Salesforce on my resume even if I used it passively?
faq.a: Yes, but be honest about your depth. 'Salesforce (pipeline management, forecasting)' is better than just 'Salesforce' because it shows what you actually did. If you created custom fields, ran reports, or trained others, call that out—it differentiates you.

faq.q: How many years of experience do I need to land a BD Manager role?
faq.a: Most hiring managers prefer 2–3 years of relevant experience (sales, account management, or junior BD), but the bar varies by company stage. Startups may hire 1-year professionals with strong results; enterprise roles often require 5+ years. Your revenue impact and deal size matter more than tenure.
